rules.title: Slovak Cardinal Numbers - Overview
rules.text: Slovak cardinal numbers (základné číslovky) express quantity and answer the question 'koľko?' (how many?). They behave differently based on the number: 'jeden' (one) declines like an adjective with full gender agreement, 'dva/tri/štyri' (2-4) have limited declension with personal/animate distinctions, and numbers 5+ decline through all cases but don't show gender. Cardinals govern the case of nouns they modify.
examples: jeden muž (one man - nominative singular)
dva muži (two men - nominative plural)
päť mužov (five men - genitive plural)
Vidím jedného muža (I see one man - accusative)
S piatimi mužmi (with five men - instrumental)

rules.title: Cardinal Number 'jeden' (one) - Complete Declension
rules.text: The number 'jeden' (one) is unique among cardinals because it declines like an adjective with full gender agreement across all three genders. It's used for counting and also functions like an indefinite article (a/an). It shows animate/inanimate distinction in masculine accusative.
examples: Nominative: Jeden muž je tu - One man is here
Nominative: Jedna žena prišla - One woman came
Nominative: Jedno dieťa spí - One child is sleeping
Genitive: Cena jedného domu - The price of one house
Genitive: Cena jednej knihy - The price of one book
Dative: Dal som to jednému mužovi - I gave it to one man
Accusative: Vidím jedného muža - I see one man (animate)
Accusative: Vidím jeden dom - I see one house (inanimate)
Accusative: Vidím jednu ženu - I see one woman
Locative: V jednom dome - In one house
Instrumental: S jedným mužom - With one man
Instrumental: S jednou ženou - With one woman

rules.title: Cardinal Number 'dva' (two) - Complete Declension
rules.text: 'Dva' (two) has special forms that distinguish between masculine personal (dvaja), masculine non-personal (dva), and feminine/neuter (dve). In oblique cases, it shows animate/inanimate distinction in accusative but otherwise has uniform endings.
examples: Nominative: Dvaja muži prišli - Two men came (personal)
Nominative: Dva domy - Two houses (non-personal)
Nominative: Dve ženy - Two women
Nominative: Dve deti - Two children
Genitive: Cena dvoch domov - The price of two houses
Genitive: Cena dvoch kníh - The price of two books
Dative: Dvom mužom - To two men
Accusative: Vidím dvoch mužov - I see two men (animate)
Accusative: Vidím dva domy - I see two houses (inanimate)
Accusative: Vidím dve ženy - I see two women
Locative: O dvoch mužoch - About two men
Instrumental: S dvoma mužmi - With two men
Instrumental: S dvoma ženami - With two women

rules.title: Cardinal Number 'tri' (three) - Complete Declension
rules.text: 'Tri' (three) distinguishes between personal (traja) and non-personal (tri) in nominative. In accusative, it shows animate/inanimate distinction. Other cases have uniform endings for all genders.
examples: Nominative: Traja študenti - Three students (personal)
Nominative: Tri autá - Three cars (non-personal)
Nominative: Tri ženy - Three women
Genitive: Cena troch áut - The price of three cars
Dative: Trom študentom - To three students
Accusative: Vidím troch študentov - I see three students (animate)
Accusative: Vidím tri autá - I see three cars (inanimate)
Locative: O troch študentoch - About three students
Instrumental: S troma študentmi - With three students

rules.title: Cardinal Number 'štyri' (four) - Complete Declension
rules.text: 'Štyri' (four) follows the same pattern as 'tri' with personal/non-personal distinction in nominative and animate/inanimate in accusative. Note the instrumental ending -mi (not -ma like dva/tri).
examples: Nominative: Štyria chlapci - Four boys (personal)
Nominative: Štyri domy - Four houses (non-personal)
Nominative: Štyri ženy - Four women
Genitive: Cena štyroch domov - The price of four houses
Dative: Štyrom chlapcom - To four boys
Accusative: Vidím štyroch chlapcov - I see four boys (animate)
Accusative: Vidím štyri domy - I see four houses (inanimate)
Locative: O štyroch chlapcoch - About four boys
Instrumental: So štyrmi chlapcami - With four boys

rules.title: Cardinal Numbers 5-10 - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: Numbers from 5 onwards decline through all cases but don't distinguish gender or animacy. They follow a consistent pattern with genitive/locative forms ending in -ich and instrumental in -imi.
examples: Nominative: Päť mužov - Five men
Genitive: Cena piatich domov - The price of five houses
Dative: Piatim mužom - To five men
Accusative: Vidím päť mužov - I see five men
Locative: O piatich mužoch - About five men
Instrumental: S piatimi mužmi - With five men
Šesť žien - Six women
Sedem detí - Seven children
Osem áut - Eight cars
Deväť kníh - Nine books
Desať rokov - Ten years

rules.title: Cardinal Numbers 11-20 - Complete Declension Table
rules.text: Numbers 11-20 are formed by adding '-násť' to the base number. They decline like numbers 5-10 with the same -ich/-im/-imi pattern in oblique cases.
examples: Nominative: Jedenásť mužov - Eleven men
Genitive: Cena dvanástich domov - The price of twelve houses
Dative: Trinástim študentom - To thirteen students
Accusative: Vidím štrnásť áut - I see fourteen cars
Locative: O pätnástich knihách - About fifteen books
Instrumental: S šestnástimi ľuďmi - With sixteen people

rules.title: Cardinal Numbers 20-100 - Tens Declension
rules.text: Tens (20, 30, 40, etc.) decline like numbers 5+. They follow the same -ich/-im/-imi pattern in oblique cases.
examples: Nominative: Dvadsať mužov - Twenty men
Genitive: Cena tridsiatich domov - The price of thirty houses
Dative: Štyridsiatim študentom - To forty students
Accusative: Vidím päťdesiat áut - I see fifty cars
Locative: O šesťdesiatich knihách - About sixty books
Instrumental: So sedemdesiatimi ľuďmi - With seventy people

rules.title: Cardinal Numbers 100, 1000 - Declension
rules.text: 'Sto' (hundred) and 'tisíc' (thousand) decline through all cases. 'Sto' has irregular forms, while 'tisíc' follows a more regular pattern.

rows: cena sta domov (price of hundred houses)
vidím sto mužov (I see hundred men)
cena tisíc domov (price of thousand houses)
vidím tisíc mužov (I see thousand men)

examples: Sto ľudí prišlo - One hundred people came
Cena sta kníh - The price of one hundred books
Tisíc vojakov - One thousand soldiers
S tisíc ľuďmi - With one thousand people

rules.title: Compound Numbers 21-99 - Formation and Declension
rules.text: Compound numbers (21-99) are formed by combining tens and units. Only the last element (units) declines; the tens part remains in nominative. The noun case is governed by the final element.
examples: Nominative: Dvadsaťjeden muž - Twenty-one men (singular!)
Nominative: Dvadsaťdva muži - Twenty-two men (plural)
Nominative: Dvadsaťpäť mužov - Twenty-five men (genitive plural)
Genitive: Cena dvadsaťjedného domu - Price of 21 houses
Genitive: Cena dvadsaťdvoch domov - Price of 22 houses
Instrumental: S dvadsaťjeden mužmi - With 21 men (only 'jeden' declines)
Mám tridsaťpäť rokov - I am 35 years old

rules.title: Cardinal Numbers - Noun Case Agreement Rules
rules.text: Cardinals govern the case of the noun they modify. The rules differ based on the number and the case of the numeral itself. This is one of the most important aspects of Slovak cardinal usage.
examples: Nominative: jeden muž, dva muži, päť mužov
Genitive: jedného muža, dvoch mužov, piatich mužov
Dative: jednému mužovi, dvom mužom, piatim mužom
Accusative: jedného muža, dvoch mužov, päť mužov
Locative: o jednom mužovi, o dvoch mužoch, o piatich mužoch
Instrumental: s jedným mužom, s dvoma mužmi, s piatimi mužmi

rules.title: Cardinals with Different Noun Genders
rules.text: Cardinals interact differently with masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns. Only 'jeden' and 'dva' show gender distinctions. Numbers 3+ use the same form for all genders.
examples: Masculine: jeden muž, dva muži, päť mužov
Feminine: jedna žena, dve ženy, päť žien
Neuter: jedno dieťa, dve deti, päť detí
Masculine personal: dvaja muži, traja študenti, štyria chlapci
Masculine non-personal: dva domy, tri stromy, štyri stoly

rules.title: Cardinals as Nouns - Substantive Use
rules.text: Cardinals can function as nouns (substantives) when they stand alone without modifying another noun. They decline according to their normal patterns and typically refer to people or things previously mentioned.
examples: Jeden z nich prišiel - One of them came
Dvaja odišli, traja zostali - Two left, three stayed
Päť ich bolo - There were five (of them)
Hovoril som s dvoma - I spoke with two (people)
Videl som troch - I saw three (people)
Jeden je dobrý, druhý zlý - One is good, the other bad
Z piatich prišli len traja - Of five, only three came
Potrebujem ešte jedného - I need one more (person)

rules.title: Cardinals in Time Expressions
rules.text: Cardinals are used extensively in time expressions for hours, years, ages, and durations. They govern genitive plural for nouns of time measurement.
examples: Je jedna hodina - It's one o'clock
Sú dve hodiny - It's two o'clock
Je päť hodín - It's five o'clock
Mám dvadsaťjeden rokov - I am 21 years old
Čakal som tri hodiny - I waited three hours
Päť dní v týždni - Five days a week
V roku 2026 - In the year 2026
O druhej hodine - At two o'clock (locative)

rules.title: Cardinals in Dates
rules.text: Cardinals are used for years in dates. Days use ordinals in genitive case. Months are in genitive case when expressing dates.
examples: Prvého januára 2026 - On the first of January 2026 (ordinal + cardinal)
Dvadsiateho druhého marca - On the twenty-second of March (ordinal)
V roku 2026 - In the year 2026 (cardinal)
Rok 2026 - The year 2026
Dvadsaťšiesteho decembra - On the twenty-sixth of December
Narodil sa v roku tisíc deväťsto deväťdesiatpäť - He was born in 1995

rules.title: Cardinals with Measurements and Quantities
rules.text: Cardinals are used with units of measurement, currency, and quantities. The noun takes genitive plural with numbers 5+.
examples: Päť kilometrov - Five kilometers
Tri kilogramy - Three kilograms
Dva litre mlieka - Two liters of milk
Desať eur - Ten euros
Dvadsať dolárov - Twenty dollars
Päť stupňov Celzia - Five degrees Celsius
Sto percent - One hundred percent
Päťdesiat percent - Fifty percent

rules.title: Collective Numerals
rules.text: Slovak has special collective numerals for groups, pairs, and kinds. These are formed differently from regular cardinals and have specific uses.
